articles.title: Code is Cheap(er)
articles.summary: Code is Cheap(er) Carson Gross June 04, 2026 Code has gotten much cheaper to create in the last year thanks to AI tools that can generate reasonably decent code quickly. This means understanding code has become more expensive as it now requires reading and analyzing generated code rather than writing from scratch. As a result, developers need to still understand the underlying code, even if it's generated by an LLM, to avoid potential dangers such as producing ununderstandable code or introducing new semantics into a code base.

articles.title: Do transformers need three projections? Systematic study of QKV variants
articles.summary: Transformers with three projections, such as QKV variants, have become a standard solution for various AI tasks but the individual contribution and impact of these projections remain poorly understood. A systematic study evaluated three projection sharing constraints: Q-K=V, Q=K-V, and Q=K=V, exploring asymmetric attention via 2D positional encodings to address symmetry in attention maps. The results showed that projection sharing can achieve significant benefits for language modeling, with Q-K=V preserving quality while breaking attention directionality, and that combining Q-K=V with GQA-4 yields substantial cache reduction and improved inference memory.

articles.title: IPv6 zones in URLs are a mistake
articles.summary: IPv6 zones in URLs are a mistake because they can cause confusion when trying to disambiguate host and port numbers, especially with URL encoding. This is due to the way IPv6 addresses are represented as part of the hostname, which can lead to errors when parsing URLs. To work around this issue, percent-encoding the percent sign in the IPv6 zone is necessary, but there's no guidance on how to properly handle it for URLs in RFC 9884 and other frameworks.

articles.title: Gaussian Point Splatting
articles.summary: Gaussian point splatting is a novel method to render large-scale scenes with many Gaussians, achieved through sampling pixel-sized points from the Gaussians and splattering them to a framebuffer using 64-bit atomics. This approach enables an even distribution of workload across millions of threads, allowing for hundreds of millions of Gaussians to be rendered in real time. By employing hierarchical frustum and occlusion culling, Gaussian point splatting can handle scenes with many different types of objects and lighting conditions.
